natural_occurrence.description: A synthetic derivative of indigo created through sulfonation. While the parent compound indigo exists naturally in plants, the water-soluble sulfonated form (E132) does not occur in nature. Produces blue colouration.

manufacturing.description: Traditional method: Indigo is manufactured by fusion of N-phenylglycine (prepared from aniline + formaldehyde) in molten mixture of sodamide, sodium hydroxide, and potassium hydroxide under ammonia pressure. Purified indigo undergoes aromatic sulfonation to introduce sulfonic acid groups at 5,5' positions, creating water solubility. Converted to disodium salt form. Modern method: Can be produced biotechnologically using genetically modified microorganisms.

other_uses.industrial: Textile dyeing (limited); laboratory pH indicator and research stain
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Tablet/capsule coatings; medical diagnostic procedures (cystoscopy, brain surgery, urological surgery, sentinel lymph node biopsy)

eu.max_level_mg_kg: varies by food category (50+ categories with quantum satis or specific limits)
eu.conditions: Must carry warning: 'May have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children' (since 2010); must meet 93% purity standard

usa.notes: Approved for food use; FDA approved for medical diagnostic use in July 2022

japan.notes: Approved as designated synthetic colour additive

regulatory_history.event: JECFA established ADI of 0-5 mg/kg body weight per day
EU mandated warning label for effects on children's activity and attention (despite not being part of Southampton Six)
EFSA re-evaluation confirmed ADI of 5 mg/kg bw/day with 93% purity requirement
JECFA 86th Report confirmed ADI; emphasized purity standards
FDA approved indigo carmine for medical diagnostic use (cystoscopy, urological procedures) in July 2022
EFSA follow-up evaluation on purity and manufacturing standards

commonly_found_in: Blue raspberry flavoured candies and ice pops
Medical: Brain surgery (stereotactic tumor margin marking)

adi_context.source_note: Based on JECFA/EFSA ADI of 5 mg/kg bw/day with 93% purity requirement. ADI applies to material manufactured using processes comparable to studies by Borzelleca (1985-1986). One of few food dyes with major medical applications.
